trajectory_summary: The call opened on a false premise — Bucky was pitching the address on file, Eric's interest was in a future home he doesn't yet own. Eric disclosed this within 30 seconds. Bucky spent the next three minutes pitching the wrong house to a customer who had already told him why that pitch wouldn't land. The call ends with a polite goodbye and zero pipeline captured. Energy did not build; it leaked steadily from the moment Eric reframed the call's scope.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown.' The correct characterization is: no appointment booked, no pipeline captured. A more precise outcome tag would be 'future_pipeline_missed' — Eric is a qualified future buyer (homeowner, interested in solar for a new construction, researched the category, asked specific product questions) who left the call with no follow-up mechanism. This outcome category does not currently exist in the schema outcomes schema; flagging as a schema gap for Kenny's review.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: my question around solar is gonna be not with that house. It's gonna be a house that I don't currently have.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Eric told Bucky the real opportunity within 30 seconds of the call starting. Bucky heard it, acknowledged it briefly, and then spent three minutes pitching the wrong house. The priority coaching beat is not objection handling or bill anchoring — it is teaching Bucky to recognize when a lead's value is future-pipeline rather than current-sit, and to have a move for that scenario. No pipeline-capture language was spoken on this call. Eric is a qualified, informed buyer who will purchase solar for a new home — Ion has no mechanism to reach him when that moment arrives.

key_moments.quote: solar shingles. And Yeah. I was does that so do you guys do that?
key_moments.stewart_read: Eric is not a cold lead. He has researched solar product categories, identified a specific product (solar shingles / integrated roof tiles), and is asking a direct qualification question. This is a buyer signal for a future purchase, not a casual inquiry. The enthusiasm is real — it just points at a future home, not the current address.

key_moments.quote: So those they're we're, like, we have those on order, but they don't come out for, like, another couple years.
key_moments.stewart_read: Solar shingles (integrated roof tiles) are explicitly listed under ion_does_NOT_sell: tesla_solar_roof_tiles. Bucky's answer — 'we have those on order, couple years out' — is factually incorrect per the schema; Ion does not carry and is not receiving Tesla solar roof tiles. However, the honest direction of the answer (we don't have them yet) is correct. The gap is not a scope-creep failure — it is a product-knowledge gap that also missed the bridge: Ion does install its own solar panels on new construction, and the closer conversation is where product options get laid out. Bucky had an opening to say 'we don't do the integrated shingle product, but our panels work on new builds — let me get you to a specialist who can show you what that looks like for your future home.'

key_moments.quote: I'm probably looking to move probably in about three years, so I don't necessarily wanna do that investment on that right at this point in time.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is a flip opportunity that Bucky walked past. Eric has just handed Bucky a time-anchored objection: 'three years, not worth the investment.' The inversion available here is not about the current house — it is about the future house. Eric is telling Bucky he is planning a significant housing move and is already thinking about solar for the next property. The flip is not 'you should do it on this house anyway' (which is what Bucky attempted) — it is 'three years is exactly the right time to start understanding what you want, so you can build it into the new home from day one.'

key_moments.quote: I've I've looked at that. I know that you guys people offer that. But talked with my realtor, and he advised me not to do that if I'm looking to sell in the near future
key_moments.stewart_read: Eric is not changing his mind — he never had a mind to change on the current house. He is citing a trusted third-party advisor (his realtor) who has already counseled against the bill-swap path. This is not a rebuttals.changed_mind scenario. It is a third-party authority objection with no direct schema rebuttal. Bucky's response ('Okay. Yeah. And you know about the installation and all that? No cost?') attempts to add a new data point (no-cost installation) but does not acknowledge the realtor's authority or engage with Eric's actual concern. The realtor's advice is reasonable and Eric has internalized it — arguing around it without acknowledging it reads as dismissive.
